description: Why do 30% of emails bounce on new domains? A quick MX record lookup reveals the hidden configuration gap holding back your deliverability.

schemas.headline: DNS Lookup Tool: Query A, AAAA, MX, CNAME, TXT & NS Records
schemas.description: Why do 30% of emails bounce on new domains? A quick MX record lookup reveals the hidden configuration gap holding back your deliverability. Check DNS records now.

mainEntity.name: What is a DNS lookup and how does it work?

acceptedAnswer.text: A DNS lookup is the process of querying the Domain Name System to find the IP address or other records associated with a domain name. It acts like the phonebook of the internet, translating human-readable names into machine-readable IPs.

mainEntity.name: Why should I use a DNS checker?

acceptedAnswer.text: Using a DNS checker helps you verify that your domain is pointing to the correct servers after a migration, troubleshoot website downtime, and ensure your email authentication (like SPF and DMARC) is properly configured.

mainEntity.name: How do I check DNS records for my domain?

acceptedAnswer.text: You can check DNS records by entering your domain into our DNS lookup tool above. Select the specific record type (such as A, TXT, or MX) you want to query, and the tool will fetch the live data from multiple global resolvers.

mainEntity.name: What is an MX record lookup used for?

acceptedAnswer.text: An MX record lookup is used to identify the mail servers responsible for receiving email on behalf of a domain. It's essential for diagnosing email delivery issues and ensuring your mail hosting is correctly linked to your domain.

mainEntity.name: How long does DNS propagation take?

acceptedAnswer.text: DNS propagation typically takes anywhere from a few minutes to 48 hours. The time depends on the Time To Live (TTL) settings of your previous records and how frequently ISPs refresh their DNS caches.
